title: Fossil Fuel 'Transition': The World Summit and the $9.75 Billion Contradiction
content:
## Fossil Fuel 'Transition': The World Summit and the $9.75 Billion Contradiction

Fifty-seven countries called it historic.

The Santa Marta conference — the first International Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels — closed in late April with language that would suggest the world had turned a page. Colombia's minister declared fossil fuels bring death. The Netherlands framed transition as an economic and security imperative. The message from the podium: ambition has become action.

The same week, a Louisiana LNG export terminal took its Final Investment Decision.

Commonwealth LNG announced $9.75 billion in backing from Mubadala Energy, Kimmeridge, and CPP Investments. That project is not a plan. It is not an aspiration. It is a signed contract for new fossil fuel infrastructure that will operate for decades.

The Santa Marta summit produced no binding targets. No mandatory timelines. No enforcement mechanisms. It was explicitly described as a "non-negotiation forum." Workstreams are voluntary. Countries are "invited via email" to report their fossil fuel subsidies. The summit's own Action Insights report called for halting all new fossil fuel expansion. Governments present did not act on that recommendation.

Meanwhile, oil prices surged 3% the same week the conference closed, driven by Hormuz war risk. Australia announced hydrogen funding going predominantly to gas-derived hydrogen. The rhetorical urgency and the capital reality have not converged.

**The gap:**
- Santa Marta language: "fossil fuels bring death"
- Simultaneous new LNG investment: $9.75 billion
- Santa Marta output: voluntary, non-binding, no enforcement
- New LNG FID: contractual, built, decades of operation

They called for an end to new fossil fuel expansion. Then signed off on $9.75 billion for new LNG. The language moved faster than the capital.
